title: Houskové knedlíky
subtitle: Czech bread dumplings
excerpt: Stale white bread, eggs, and flour bound together and shaped into rolls, then boiled until light and yielding. The essential side dish of Czech cooking — sliced thick and served to absorb the sauce from goulash or svíčková.
sourceNotes: Based on the Czech houskové knedlíky tradition as documented in Jiri Kopac and Pavel Maurer, 'Czech Cuisine' (1999). Bread dumplings are the defining side dish of Czech cooking, pairing with virtually every braised meat and sauce preparation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice, cool, and freeze. Reheat by steaming for 10 minutes or microwaving briefly under a damp cloth.

content.text: Czech knedlíky are not the heavy doughy spheres of some dumpling traditions. Made correctly, they are light, slightly bouncy, and slice cleanly — with a crumb that opens up to absorb whatever sauce is underneath. The key ingredient is day-old or stale white bread: too fresh and the dumpling is gluey; too dry and it stays dense. The dough should hold its shape when formed but feel soft, not stiff.

The dumplings are formed into short cylinders, wrapped in a clean cloth or cling film, and boiled whole, then sliced just before serving. The slicing is part of the presentation: thick rounds, cut with a sharp knife, laid alongside the meat and sauce.

items.prepNote: stale or day-old, crusts removed, torn into small pieces
fresh, finely chopped — optional but traditional

content.text: Put {{white-bread}} of stale white bread pieces in a large bowl. Pour {{whole-milk}} of warm milk over and leave for 5 minutes until the bread softens and absorbs the milk. Add {{eggs}} beaten eggs, {{plain-flour}} of flour, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. If using, add {{parsley}} of chopped parsley. Mix with your hands or a spoon until a cohesive, slightly sticky dough forms. It should hold its shape when squeezed.

Divide the dough in half. Using wet or lightly floured hands, shape each half into a firm cylinder about 6–7 cm in diameter and 15 cm long. Wrap each cylinder tightly in a square of clean damp cloth or doubled cling film, twisting the ends to secure. Lower both rolls into a large pan of well-salted boiling water. Boil for 20–25 minutes, turning once halfway through. Lift out and rest for 10 minutes before unwrapping.
Cut each roll into 2 cm slices with a sharp knife or a taut length of thread drawn through the roll. Serve immediately alongside goulash or any braised meat with a generous sauce.

items.cause: Too much flour, or bread too fresh
items.fix: The bread should be stale enough to absorb the milk fully. Use the minimum amount of flour — just enough to bring the dough together.

items.symptom: Dumplings fall apart in the water
items.cause: Wrapping not tight enough, or water boiling too vigorously
items.fix: Wrap very tightly in the cloth or cling film and ensure the ends are properly sealed. Keep the water at a steady boil, not a rolling boil.

items.symptom: Sticky or gluey texture when sliced
items.fix: A cooked dumpling feels firm when pressed through the cloth. Rest for the full 10 minutes before slicing — the interior continues to set as it cools slightly.

content.text: Knedlíky are so central to Czech cooking that the country's food culture is sometimes summarised as guláš s knedlíky — goulash with dumplings. The bread dumpling (houskový knedlík) and the potato dumpling (bramborový knedlík) are the two standard forms, each suited to different dishes. Bread dumplings, lighter and more absorbent, pair with the rich braised sauces of everyday Czech cooking. They appear at every hospoda, every family Sunday lunch, and on every table where something has been braised long and slowly.
